reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar cycloaddition of phenylacetylene with ethyl nitroacetate to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate
bond_formed: C–O and C–C bonds in isoxazole ring
bond_broken: N–O bond rearrangement in nitrile oxide formation; loss of H2O from nitroacetate

feed_streams.reasoning: Substrate (dipolarophile) dissolved in DES; separate from nitroacetate to control mixing and minimize premature nitrile oxide dimerization

reagents: ethyl nitroacetate (2.0 equiv) in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
feed_streams.reasoning: 1,3-Dipole precursor dissolved in DES; kept separate until T-mixer to ensure rapid, homogeneous mixing at reactor entrance

reagents.name: phenylacetylene
reagents.notes: Terminal alkyne; reacts regioselectively with nitrile oxide to give 5-phenylisoxazole

reagents.name: ethyl nitroacetate
reagents.notes: Generates nitrile oxide in situ via thermal dehydration; 2 equiv used to drive conversion

reagents.name: tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB)
reagents.notes: Forms deep eutectic solvent with ethylene glycol; acts as reaction medium and may stabilize dipolar intermediates

reagents.name: ethylene glycol (EG)
reagents.equiv_or_loading: 1:5 molar ratio (TBAB:EG), ~1 mL total DES per 0.5 mmol substrate
reagents.notes: Hydrogen bond donor in DES; high boiling point enables 120°C operation

mechanism_steps.description: Thermal dehydration/tautomerization of ethyl nitroacetate to generate ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (nitrile oxide intermediate) in situ
Concerted [3+2] cycloaddition of the nitrile oxide (1,3-dipole) with phenylacetylene (dipolarophile) to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate regioselectively

key_intermediate: ethoxycarbonylformonitrile oxide (nitrile oxide from ethyl nitroacetate)

stream_logic.reasoning: Dipolarophile in DES; kept separate to ensure controlled mixing with nitrile oxide precursor at reactor inlet
Nitrile oxide precursor in DES; separate stream allows precise stoichiometric control via flow rate ratio (2:1 equiv ratio achieved by concentration × flow rate)

mixing_order_reasoning: Phenylacetylene and ethyl nitroacetate must be mixed rapidly at the reactor entrance via a T-mixer or static mixer. The nitrile oxide intermediate generated from ethyl nitroacetate is transient and prone to dimerization (furoxan formation) if not immediately trapped by the alkyne. Rapid, efficient mixing ensures the nitrile oxide encounters phenylacetylene before dimerization can occur. Both streams are in the same DES solvent, so miscibility is not an issue.
deoxygenation_reasoning: No oxygen-sensitive species or radicals involved; the reaction proceeds via a concerted pericyclic mechanism under air.
quench_reasoning: The batch protocol quenches with water only for workup/extraction purposes, not to stop a dangerous reaction. In flow, the product stream can be collected directly and worked up offline.
wavelength_reasoning: This is a purely thermal reaction; no photocatalyst or photochemical activation is involved.
confidence_notes: High confidence in mechanism: well-established nitrile oxide [3+2] cycloaddition. The exact mechanism of nitrile oxide generation from ethyl nitroacetate in DES (thermal dehydration vs. base-mediated) is assumed to be purely thermal given no base/catalyst is added. DES viscosity (~50–100 cP at RT, lower at 120°C) may require wider tubing or higher pump pressures; recommend 1.0 mm ID PFA coil with back-pressure regulator at 8–10 bar. The 2.0 equiv of ethyl nitroacetate suggests some nitrile oxide is lost to dimerization — flow's improved mixing could allow reducing to 1.5 equiv. SMILES for TBAB includes counterion for clarity.

intensification_mandate.flow_justification_basis: Flow provides three key advantages: (1) Superior heat transfer in narrow-bore tubing (e.g., 1 mm ID PFA coil) enables rapid heating to 120°C, reducing thermal lag and potentially allowing higher temperatures (e.g., 140–150°C) under back-pressure to cut residence time from 15 min to ~5 min. (2) The transient nitrile oxide intermediate is generated and consumed continuously, minimizing its steady-state concentration and suppressing furoxan dimerization — this should improve yield beyond 83%. (3) The DES is viscous; thin-film flow in narrow tubing improves mass transfer vs. bulk stirring. Back-pressure regulation (e.g., 5–10 bar) keeps the DES and volatile phenylacetylene (bp 144°C) in liquid phase at elevated temperatures.
